1: ha-mishkan (hah-mish-KAN): 'the tabernacle' — now the structure itself is described. yeriot (ye-ri-OT): 'curtains/sheets' (H3407). shesh moshzar (shesh mo-SHAR): 'twisted fine linen' (H8336 H7806 — braided/twisted). ma'aseh choshev (mah-ah-SEH kho-SHEV): 'work of a skilled craftsman/designer' (H4639 H2803 — from chashav = to think/devise/plan/weave). The highest grade of weaving — the curtains that form the inner skin of the tabernacle are the product of intentional design, skilled execution, and artistic thought. The same root chashav means to think or reckon — the tabernacle is a thought made fabric.
2: The dimensions of each inner curtain: 28 cubits long and 4 cubits wide — approximately 14m × 2m. All ten curtains identical in size: mida achat lechol hayeriot — one measure for all the curtains. The uniformity signals that there is no hierarchy among the curtains themselves; only their assembly into joined sets creates structure.
3: Five curtains joined to one another, and five joined to one another — two sets of five. The joining (chabar H2266 — to unite/couple) creates two large panels that are then clasped together to form the whole tabernacle ceiling. The number five appears repeatedly in the tabernacle — five curtains, five pillars at the entrance — and may echo the Pentateuch structure or the five fingers of the priestly hand.
4: lulot tekhelet (loo-LOT te-kheh-LET): 'loops of blue' (H3924 H8504). The loops are made along the edge of the last curtain in each set — fifty loops on each edge, positioned to align precisely with the loops on the opposite set. The blue (tekhelet) of the loops marks this joining edge as sacred — the same blue as the priests' garments and the tzitzit thread.
5: chamishim lulot (kha-mish-EEM loo-LOT): 'fifty loops' on each set — the total join has 50 pairs of loops. Fifty is significant: the Jubilee year is the 50th year (Leviticus 25:10), and the Feast of Weeks (Shavuot/Pentecost) is the 50th day after Passover. The tabernacle's internal structure carries the number of divine release and covenant renewal.
6: klasei zahav (klah-SEI zah-HAV): 'golden clasps' (H7165 H2091). Fifty gold clasps join the two halves of the inner tabernacle into one: vehayah hamishkan echad — 'and the tabernacle shall be one.' The architectural unity of the sanctuary reflects the oneness of its God. Fifty gold clasps, 50 bronze clasps for the outer covering — the same number unifies the sacred dwelling at both layers.
7: yeriot izim (ye-ri-OT iz-ZEEM): 'curtains of goat hair' (H3407 H5795). The second layer of covering — eleven curtains of goat hair over the inner linen curtains. Goat hair was used for tent fabric throughout the ancient Near East — waterproof and durable. The outer layers protect the inner; the sacred linen curtains with cherubim are never exposed to the elements.
8: The goat hair curtains are slightly larger than the inner linen ones: 30 cubits long (vs. 28) and 4 cubits wide. The extra length allows for overhang. Eleven curtains vs. the inner ten — the outer covering is more extensive, wrapping further around the sacred structure. The slight excess is architectural wisdom for desert conditions.
9: The sixth curtain is to be folded double at the front of the tent — the doubled curtain creates extra thickness at the entrance, where wear and exposure are greatest. The arrangement reveals practical engineering within the sacred commission: durability serves holiness. The doubled front also creates a visual marker of the entrance.
10: Fifty loops on the edge of the last outer curtain, aligned to match the inner curtains' loops. The same 50-loop structure — the outer goat hair covering mirrors the inner linen covering in its joining system. The tabernacle is built by correspondence: inner and outer, above and below, holy and most holy are all parallel structures.
11: klasei nechoshet (klah-SEI ne-KHO-shet): 'bronze clasps' (H7165 H5178). Bronze for the outer goat hair covering, gold for the inner linen curtains — the decreasing value of metals as one moves outward reflects the graduated holiness of the tabernacle zones: gold (most holy/holy places), silver (sockets/bases), bronze (outer court). Even the clasps encode the theology of access.
12: The half-curtain surplus from the odd eleventh curtain hangs down over the back of the tabernacle. Nothing is wasted in the divine design — the extra curtain creates additional back coverage. The instruction reveals that the outer covering extends beyond the inner curtains on all sides, protecting the sacred structure completely.
13: A cubit of extra length hangs down on each side of the tabernacle. The outer covering is longer than the inner by two cubits total — one cubit of overhang per side. The inner curtains are fully enclosed within the outer covering. This architectural detail reinforces the principle: holiness is surrounded, protected, and approached only through layers of mediation.
14: ore eilim meaddamim (oh-ROT ay-LEEM me-ad-dah-MEEM): 'rams' skins dyed red' (H5785 H352 H119, from adam = red/ruddy — same root as adam/human and edom/red). ore tachashim: tachash skins (H8476). The two outermost layers are utilitarian coverings — water-resistant animal skins. The innermost layer is gold and cherubim; the outermost is animal hide. The tabernacle is wrapped in mortality to enclose the divine presence — a structural parable of incarnation.
15: kerashim (ke-rah-SHEEM): 'planks/boards/frames' (H7175, singular keresh). The upright wooden framework of the tabernacle — 48 boards total forming the three solid walls. atzei shittim omedim: 'standing/upright acacia planks.' Omedim from amad (to stand H5975) — the same word for those who stand in divine service. The planks stand upright before YHWH.
16: Each board: eser ammot orech haqqeresh — ten cubits long (ca. 4.5m), and one and a half cubits wide (amah va-chatzi). The height of the boards (ten cubits) determines the interior height of the tabernacle — ten cubits of standing room for the worship of the Holy One. Ten is the number of the Decalogue; the house of the commandments stands ten cubits tall.
17: shetei yadot laqqeresh echad (she-TEI yah-DOT la-KE-resh eh-KHAD): 'two tenons/hands for each board' (H3027). Yadot is the plural of yad — hand. The mortise-tenon joints by which each board is inserted into its silver base are called hands. The boards are planted into the earth by their hands — anthropomorphic building language. Every board has two hands reaching down into the silver sockets that hold it. The architecture uses human body vocabulary: the tabernacle has hands and feet.
18: The south side (negev H5045 — same word as the Negev desert) receives twenty boards. The full south wall. lemiphat negev teymana: 'for the south side, southward' — the southern exposure of the desert tabernacle, facing the prevailing winds and maximum sun. Twenty boards for the longest walls.
19: arbaim adnei kesef (ar-bah-EEM ad-NEI KEH-sef): 'forty silver sockets/bases' (H134 H3701). Two sockets per board, under each of the two tenons. The silver sockets are the foundation of the entire tabernacle structure — the boards are not set into the ground but into cast silver. The number of silver sockets (96 total for the walls plus 4 for the veil = 100) connects to the 100 silver talents contributed as the silver redemption money counted in Ex Exodus 38:25-28. The foundation of the tabernacle literally rests on the ransom silver of the people.
20: The north side: twenty boards — matching the south side exactly. The tabernacle is symmetrical: north and south walls identical in board count, dimensions, and silver socket foundations. This bilateral symmetry reflects the orderliness of the divine dwelling — nothing is haphazard, everything corresponds.
21: Forty silver sockets for the north side — two per board, twenty boards. The total for the two long walls: 80 silver sockets, foundation of 40 boards. The silver foundation runs the full length of both long walls, unbroken. The most holy place rests on silver as surely as the outer court does — the entire sanctuary has a unified silver foundation.
22: ulirkete ha-mishkan yamah (oo-lir-ke-TEI hah-mish-KAN yah-MAH): 'and for the rear of the tabernacle westward' (yam = sea, the west — Israel orients by the Mediterranean Sea to the west). The west end gets six boards — the shorter back wall. The tabernacle faces east (entrance to the east, most holy place to the west) — oriented toward the rising sun while the divine presence dwells in the west.
23: ulirkete ha-mishkan shetei qerashot: 'and for the corners of the tabernacle at the rear — two corner boards.' miqqtso'ot: corner boards (H4742, from qatsa = corner/edge). The corner boards are a structural challenge — they must join two perpendicular walls while maintaining the uniform dimensions. The instruction that follows (Exodus 26:24) explains how they are doubled and joined at the top.
24: veyihyu toa-mim mitachat veyachdav yihyu el rosho: 'they shall be twin from below, and together they shall be at its top, to the one ring.' The corner boards are doubled — each corner has two boards joined together, creating a structural column at each rear corner. They are joined at the top by one ring, binding the two corner boards into a single unit. This engineering solution for the corners reflects sophisticated desert carpentry within the divinely revealed design.
25: Eight boards for the west end (six boards + two doubled corner boards = eight total positions). Sixteen silver sockets — two per board. shisha qereshim velirkat: six boards for the back, plus two corner boards = eight total. The west wall closes the tabernacle, sealing the most holy place at the rear.
26: berikhim atzei shittim (be-ri-KHEEM ah-TZEI shit-TEEM): 'crossbars of acacia wood' (H1280 H6086 H7848). From barach — to flee through, to pass through (H1272). The horizontal crossbars run through rings in the boards, binding the vertical boards into a unified wall. Five bars for each side — five bars hold the south wall, five the north wall, five the west wall.
27: Five crossbars for the north wall, five for the west wall — the same structural system on all three sides. The crossbars ensure that the individual boards cannot topple or separate during the desert march and reassembly. They pass through gold rings in the boards, becoming part of the gold-overlay system — even the structural engineering is consecrated.
28: haberiach hatikkon betokh haqqereshim (hah-be-ri-AKH hah-ti-KHON be-TOKH hah-keh-rah-SHEEM): 'the middle crossbar in the middle of the boards' — tikkoni = middle/central (H8484). The central bar runs the full length of the wall from end to end. This central spine of the tabernacle — running through the middle of all the boards on each wall — is the structural backbone of the divine house. The middle bar is hidden within the boards, invisible once installed, like the spine within the body.
29: The boards are overlaid with gold (tetzapeh zahav); gold rings hold the crossbars; the crossbars themselves are overlaid with gold. The entire wooden structure — boards and bars alike — is encased in gold. The wood is the structural support; the gold is the sacred surface. The incorruptible acacia provides the skeleton; the pure gold provides the glory. Nothing of the wooden structure is visible inside the tabernacle — only gold.
30: kahaqem et ha-mishkan kemishpato (kah-HA-kem et hah-mish-KAN ke-mish-PA-to): 'you shall set up the tabernacle according to its ordinance/manner' — mishpat (H4941, ordinance, right manner) here means the proper procedure/design revealed to Moses. The third reference to the revealed pattern (cf. Exodus 26:9, 40 in Exodus 25) — the assembly of the tabernacle is not improvisational. There is a right way, a divinely ordained order, for establishing the sacred dwelling.
31: parochet (pah-RO-khet): 'veil/curtain' (H6532, perhaps from parach = to separate/divide or from Akkadian baraku). The inner veil is the most theologically significant piece of fabric in the tabernacle. Same materials as the inner curtains (tekhelet/argaman/shani/shesh), with cherubim woven in by the most skilled craftsman (choshev). This veil separates the holy place from the holy of holies — the barrier between human worship and the divine throne. It is this veil that tears from top to bottom at the crucifixion of Jesus (Matthew 27:51; Mark 15:38), the torn flesh of Christ opening the way into the divine presence (Hebrews 10:19-20): a new and living way through the veil.
32: Four acacia pillars overlaid with gold, with gold hooks (vavihem zahav), set in four silver sockets (arba adnei kesef). The parochet hangs from the gold hooks on these four pillars. The pillars stand between the holy place and the most holy — the threshold of access. Four pillars: four is the number of the earth's corners, of completeness in the material realm. The silver bases again: the sacred structure rests on silver redemption.
33: vehivdilah haparochet lachem bayn haqdosh uvayn qodesh haqdoshim: 'and the veil shall divide for you between the holy place and the holy of holies.' The architectural division of the tabernacle is explicit: the parochet creates two zones — haqdosh (the holy place, outer room) and qodesh haqdoshim (the holy of holies, inner room). The veil is not merely decoration but a theological boundary: the most holy place is inaccessible to all but the high priest, once a year, on Yom Kippur. This spatial theology of graduated access is dissolved in Christ (Hebrews 9:8, 12).
34: The kapporet (atonement cover) is placed on the aron haedut (ark of the testimony) in the qodesh haqdoshim. The sequence: ark inside, kapporet on top, parochet surrounding — the most holy objects are the most enclosed. Three zones of access (outer court → holy place → holy of holies) correspond to three levels of approach: Israel, priests, high priest. The high priest alone enters the innermost room, only on one day of the year, only with blood.
35: The table of the bread of the presence is placed outside the veil on the north side; the menorah is placed opposite on the south side. These two furnishings face each other across the holy place, both outside the parochet — the bread of divine provision and the light of divine presence flanking the approach to the veil. All three furnishings of the holy place (altar of incense, table, menorah) are positioned with precise intentionality.
36: masak (mah-SAK): 'screen/curtain' (H4539, from nasak — to weave?). The screen for the entrance to the tent is woven in the same four colors as the parochet but without cherubim — the entrance has the colors of holiness but lacks the angelic guardians that mark the boundary of the most holy. The masak is an invitation; the parochet is a warning. Entry into the holy place is open to the priests; entry through the parochet is reserved for the high priest alone.
37: Five acacia pillars overlaid with gold, with gold hooks, for the entrance screen — but five bronze sockets (adnei nechoshet), not silver. The change from silver to bronze at the entrance marks the threshold between the holy place system and the outer court system: silver belongs to the inner sanctuary, bronze to the outer court. The entrance pillars stand at the exact boundary where the two material systems meet — a precise material theology of spatial holiness.
